content.title: Subsequence
content.content:
Let $(a_n)$ be a sequence. A **subsequence** of 
$(a_n)$ is a sequence $(a_{n_k})_{k=1}^{\infty}$ where 
$n_1 < n_2 < n_3 < \cdots$ is a strictly increasing sequence of natural 
numbers.

content.text:
In other words, a subsequence is obtained by selecting infinitely many 
terms from the original sequence while preserving their order.

content.title: Subsequences
content.problem:
Let $a_n = (-1)^n$. Then:

- $(a_{2k}) = (1, 1, 1, \ldots)$ is a subsequence converging to $1$.

- $(a_{2k-1}) = (-1, -1, -1, \ldots)$ is a subsequence converging to $-1$.
The original sequence diverges, but it has convergent subsequences.

content.title: Subsequence Limit Theorem
content.content: If $(a_n) \to L$, then every subsequence $(a_{n_k}) \to L$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $\varepsilon > 0$. Since $a_n \to L$, there exists $N$ such that 
$n \geq N$ implies $|a_n - L| < \varepsilon$.

Since $(n_k)$ is increasing with $n_k \geq k$ (by induction), for 
$k \geq N$ we have $n_k \geq k \geq N$, so $|a_{n_k} - L| < \varepsilon$.

content.title: Divergent Subsequences Criterion
content.content:
If a sequence has two subsequences converging to different limits, then 
the sequence diverges.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
If $(a_n)$ converged to $L$, then by Theorem [thm:subseq-limit], 
every subsequence would also converge to $L$.

content.title: Bolzano-Weierstrass Theorem
content.content: Every bounded sequence has a convergent subsequence.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $(a_n)$ be bounded, say $a_n \in [a, b]$ for all $n$.

We construct a convergent subsequence using interval bisection.

Let $I_1 = [a, b]$. Bisect $I_1$ into two equal halves. At least one half 
contains infinitely many terms of $(a_n)$; call it $I_2$. Continue: 
bisect $I_2$ and choose the half $I_3$ containing infinitely many terms. 
This produces a nested sequence of closed intervals $I_1 \supseteq I_2 \supseteq \cdots$ 
with $|I_n| = (b-a)/2^{n-1} \to 0$.

By the Nested Interval Property, $\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} I_n = \{L\}$ for 
some $L \in \mathbb{R}$.

Now choose $n_1$ so that $a_{n_1} \in I_1$. Choose $n_2 > n_1$ so that 
$a_{n_2} \in I_2$ (possible since infinitely many terms are in $I_2$). 
Continue to get a subsequence $(a_{n_k})$ with $a_{n_k} \in I_k$.

Since $a_{n_k}, L \in I_k$ and $|I_k| \to 0$, we have $|a_{n_k} - L| \to 0$, 
i.e., $a_{n_k} \to L$.

content.title: Bernard Bolzano (1781--1848) and Karl Weierstrass (1815--1897)
content.content:
Bolzano, a Bohemian priest and philosopher, discovered many fundamental 
results in analysis decades before they were widely known. Weierstrass, 
the "father of modern analysis," brought rigor to the subject and 
trained a generation of mathematicians. The theorem bearing their names 
is a cornerstone of real analysis.

content.title: Limit Superior and Inferior
content.content:
Let $(a_n)$ be a bounded sequence. Define:
$$
\limsup_{n \to \infty} a_n &= \lim_{n \to \infty} \sup\{a_k : k \geq n\} \\
\liminf_{n \to \infty} a_n &= \lim_{n \to \infty} \inf\{a_k : k \geq n\}
$$

content.text:
The $\limsup$ is the largest limit of any convergent subsequence; the 
$\liminf$ is the smallest.

content.title: Characterization of $\limsup$ and $\liminf$
content.content:
For a bounded sequence $(a_n)$:

1. $(a_n)$ converges if and only if $\limsup a_n = \liminf a_n$.

2. In this case, $\lim a_n = \limsup a_n = \liminf a_n$.
content.label: Theorem
